title: Adrenal Fatigue Critique: A Useful Frame Anyway
dek: The medical establishment says adrenal fatigue isn't real. But when your body is vibrating with exhaustion and your psoas is locked in a permanent brace, the clinical debate doesn't matter. The frame gives us a way to understand the biology of our burnout.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Clinical Dismissal


*Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T00:13:18.220Z">May 28, 2026</time>.*

You are sitting in the sterile, fluorescent-lit office of an endocrinologist. The paper on the exam table crinkles under your thighs. You have just described the way your body feels at 3:00 PM - the sudden, hollow drop in your chest, the way your masseter muscles clench so tightly your teeth ache, the sensation that your blood has been replaced by lead. You describe the wired, vibrating exhaustion that hits at 11:00 PM, keeping your eyes wide open while your bones beg for sleep. 

The doctor looks at your blood work. "Your cortisol levels are within normal limits," they say, their voice smooth and detached. "Adrenal fatigue is not a recognized medical condition. It's a myth."

They are not entirely wrong, clinically speaking. The Endocrine Society has stated unequivocally that "adrenal fatigue" is not a real disease. The adrenal glands, those two walnut-sized organs sitting atop your kidneys, do not simply "tire out" and stop producing cortisol in the way the internet wellness gurus claim. If your adrenal glands actually fail, that is Addison's disease, a life-threatening condition. What you have, the doctor implies, is stress. Or depression. Or perhaps you just need to practice better sleep hygiene.

But this clinical dismissal misses the entire point of what your body is trying to communicate. The term "adrenal fatigue" might be biologically imprecise, but as a somatic frame, it is profoundly useful. It gives a name to the systemic, bone-deep exhaustion that occurs when your nervous system has been locked in a state of sympathetic arousal for years. It acknowledges the physical reality of living in a body that has been forced to treat every email, every micro-aggression, every unspoken expectation as a mortal threat.

When we are told that our exhaustion is "just stress," it minimizes the profound physiological changes occurring within us. It turns a biological reality into a psychological failing. But your body knows the truth. Your body knows that the constant bracing of your trapezius muscles, the shallow breathing that barely moves your diaphragm, the way your gut clenches before you even open your inbox - these are not just "stress." They are the physical manifestations of a nervous system that has been pushed beyond its capacity to regulate.

Gabor Maté, in his profound work on the connection between stress and disease, reminds us that the body does not separate the emotional from the physical. In *When the Body Says No*, he writes about how chronic, hidden stress - the kind we are taught to normalize and push through - eventually manifests as physical illness. The medical establishment's refusal to recognize the systemic impact of this chronic stress leaves us without a language to describe our own lived experience. The frame of "adrenal fatigue," however imperfect, provides that language. It says: *Your exhaustion is real. Your body is not failing you; it is responding exactly as it was designed to respond to an unlivable environment.*

## The Biology of Bracing

Let us look at what is actually happening in your body when you are in this state of profound depletion. It is not that your adrenal glands are broken. It is that your entire hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis has been dysregulated by chronic, unrelenting demand.

Imagine your psoas muscle - the deep, core muscle that connects your lumbar spine to your femur. This is the muscle of fight or flight. When a threat is perceived, the psoas contracts, preparing you to run or to curl into a protective ball. In a healthy nervous system, once the threat has passed, the psoas releases. The body returns to a state of rest and digest.

But what happens when the threat never passes? What happens when the threat is not a predator, but a culture that demands constant productivity, a family dynamic that requires constant vigilance, a society that penalizes rest? The psoas never fully releases. It remains in a state of chronic, low-grade contraction. This constant bracing requires an immense amount of energy. It is like driving a car with the parking brake engaged. No wonder you are exhausted.

This is where the herbs come in. Not as a cure-all, not as a way to "fix" your broken adrenals, but as allies in the process of down-regulating your nervous system. When I work with women in this state of profound depletion, I do not reach for stimulants. I reach for the deep, nourishing adaptogens and nervines. 

Consider Ashwagandha. [Gaia Herbs Sound Sleep](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0036THNRA?tag=spankyspinola-20) is not a magic pill that will instantly restore your energy. But it is a profound ally in helping the body modulate its stress response. It works with the HPA axis, helping to smooth out the spikes and crashes of cortisol. When you take it, you might notice that the sudden, hollow drop in your chest at 3:00 PM becomes a little less severe. You might notice that the wired, vibrating energy at 11:00 PM begins to soften into a more natural tiredness.

And then there is Holy Basil, or Tulsi. [FES Yarrow Environmental Solution Spray (2-pack)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00F3SKA7M?tag=spankyspinola-20) is an herb that feels like a deep, grounding breath. It does not force the body into a state of artificial calm; rather, it helps to lift the heavy, stagnant energy of chronic stress. It reminds the nervous system that it is possible to be alert without being alarmed.

These herbs are not treating a disease called "adrenal fatigue." They are supporting a body that is caught in a loop of chronic bracing. They are offering a biological intervention for a biological reality.

## The Somatic Reality of Exhaustion

When we talk about exhaustion, we often talk about it as a lack of energy. But somatic exhaustion is not just an absence; it is a presence. It is the presence of tension, the presence of holding, the presence of unexpressed survival energy.

Notice your jaw right now. Notice the masseter muscle, the thick muscle at the angle of your jaw. Is it clenched? Is it holding the words you swallowed yesterday? Is it bracing against the expectations of tomorrow? This chronic clenching is a massive drain on your system's resources.

Bessel van der Kolk, in *The Body Keeps the Score*, articulates how trauma and chronic stress literally reshape both body and brain. He explains that when we are unable to complete a defensive response - when we cannot fight or flee - that survival energy gets trapped in the body. It manifests as chronic muscle tension, digestive issues, and, yes, profound exhaustion. The body is working overtime to contain the unexpressed energy.

This is why the clinical dismissal of adrenal fatigue is so damaging. It tells us that because our blood work is normal, our exhaustion is not real. But the exhaustion is the symptom of a body that is working incredibly hard just to maintain a baseline of functioning. The exhaustion is the cost of containment.

When you wake up at 3:00 AM, your heart racing, your mind spinning with a list of tasks, that is not a failure of your sleep hygiene. That is your sympathetic nervous system firing, perceiving a threat in the stillness of the night. Your body is doing exactly what it is supposed to do when it believes it is in danger. The problem is not your body; the problem is the constant signaling of danger.

To heal from this state of depletion, we have to stop trying to "fix" our energy levels and start addressing the underlying state of alarm. We have to begin to speak the language of the body.

## The Practice of Yielding

How do we begin to unwind this chronic bracing? How do we signal to the body that it is safe to put down the armor? It begins with the practice of yielding.

Yielding is not collapsing. Collapsing is a trauma response; it is the dorsal vagal state of shutdown, where the body simply gives up. Yielding is an active, conscious choice to release weight into the support of the earth. It is the opposite of bracing.

**An Embodied Practice: The Floor Yield**

Find a space on the floor where you will not be disturbed. Lie on your back, with your knees bent and your feet flat on the floor, hip-width apart. This is the constructive rest position. It takes the pressure off the psoas and allows the lower back to settle.

Place your hands on your lower belly, just below your navel. Do not try to change your breathing. Just notice it. Notice if the breath is shallow, trapped in the upper chest. Notice if the diaphragm feels tight or restricted.

Now, bring your attention to the points of contact between your body and the floor. Feel the back of your skull, your shoulder blades, your sacrum, the soles of your feet. 

Imagine that the floor is rising up to meet you. You do not have to hold yourself up. The earth is holding you. 

With each exhalation, consciously invite one percent more of your body weight to release into the floor. Start with your jaw. Let the lower jaw unhinge slightly. Let the tongue rest heavy in the bottom of the mouth. 

Move to your shoulders. Let the trapezius muscles melt toward the floor. 

Move to your pelvis. Let the sacrum spread wide and heavy.

Stay here for ten minutes. When your mind wanders to the email you need to send or the conversation you need to have, gently bring your attention back to the physical sensation of weight releasing into the floor. 

This practice is not a cure. It is a conversation. It is a way of telling your nervous system, *Right now, in this exact moment, we are safe. We do not need to brace.*

## The Fascial Web of Holding

When we talk about chronic tension, we often focus on the muscles - the tight shoulders, the clenched jaw. But we must also talk about the fascia. The fascia is the continuous web of connective tissue that wraps around every muscle, every organ, every nerve in your body. It is a highly innervated, sensory-rich system that responds directly to the state of your nervous system.

When you are in a state of chronic alarm, your fascia literally changes its physical properties. It becomes thicker, stickier, and less pliable. It is as if the body is weaving an internal suit of armor to protect you from the perceived threat. This fascial restriction is a major contributor to the profound sense of heaviness and exhaustion that characterizes this state of depletion.

Imagine the fascia around your diaphragm. The diaphragm is the primary muscle of respiration, a large, dome-shaped muscle that sits at the base of your ribcage. In a state of safety, the diaphragm descends fully on the inhalation, massaging the abdominal organs and drawing a deep, nourishing breath into the lower lobes of the lungs. 

But when the nervous system is locked in sympathetic arousal, the fascia around the diaphragm tightens. The breath becomes shallow, restricted to the upper chest. This shallow breathing sends a continuous feedback loop to the brain, confirming that there is a threat, which in turn keeps the nervous system in a state of alarm. It is a vicious cycle of biological bracing.

Breaking this cycle requires more than just "taking a deep breath." It requires a slow, patient unwinding of the fascial restrictions. It requires bringing conscious awareness to the areas of holding and offering them the safety they need to release.

This is where the somatic work becomes deeply intimate. It is about feeling the exact texture of the restriction. Is the tightness in your chest sharp and jagged, or is it dull and heavy? Does it feel like a tight band around your ribs, or a hard knot in your solar plexus? By getting specific about the somatic reality of the holding, we begin to differentiate the physical sensation from the emotional story. We begin to treat the body as a biological organism rather than a psychological problem.

## The Nighttime Vigilance

One of the most agonizing aspects of this profound depletion is the disruption of sleep. You are exhausted all day, dragging your body through the hours, but the moment your head hits the pillow, your nervous system snaps to attention. Your heart rate elevates, your mind races, and your body feels wired with a vibrating, electric energy.

This is not insomnia in the traditional sense. This is nighttime vigilance. 

From an evolutionary perspective, the night is a time of vulnerability. If your nervous system believes you are under threat, it will not allow you to drop into the deep, restorative states of sleep. It will keep you hovering in the lighter stages of sleep, ready to wake at the slightest sound. It will jolt you awake at 3:00 AM, the time when cortisol naturally begins to rise, interpreting this normal biological shift as a signal of danger.

The medical advice for this is often centered around "sleep hygiene" - dark rooms, no screens, cool temperatures. While these are helpful, they do not address the root cause: a nervous system that is terrified to let its guard down.

When you wake up at 3:00 AM, your chest tight and your mind spinning, the worst thing you can do is look at the clock and calculate how many hours of sleep you are losing. This only adds more alarm to the system. Instead, try to shift your focus from the goal of sleeping to the practice of resting.

Notice the physical sensations of the vigilance. Notice the way your eyes feel wide open in the dark. Notice the tension in your neck. Remind yourself, *My body is trying to protect me. It is doing its job.* 

This is where the nervines can be profoundly supportive. A cup of strong chamomile tea, made with [Gaia Herbs SleepThru](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005H1ENTQ?tag=spankyspinola-20), is not just a soothing beverage. It is a biological signal of safety. The apigenin in chamomile binds to the same receptors in the brain as anti-anxiety medications, promoting a gentle, natural relaxation of the nervous system. It is a way of whispering to your body that the night is safe.

## The Anatomy of a Boundary

When we talk about the somatic reality of adrenal fatigue, we must inevitably talk about boundaries. A boundary is not just a psychological concept; it is a physical, biological reality. It is the edge of your energetic field, the physical limit of what your nervous system can process and integrate.

When your boundaries are chronically violated - whether by a demanding boss, an emotionally draining family member, or a culture that demands constant availability - your nervous system registers this as a threat. The body responds by bracing. The muscles tighten, the breath becomes shallow, and the HPA axis floods the system with cortisol and adrenaline.

Over time, this chronic boundary violation leads to the profound depletion we call adrenal fatigue. The body simply cannot sustain the energy required to constantly defend against these intrusions. The armor becomes too heavy to carry.

Healing requires us to rebuild these biological boundaries. This is not just about learning to say "no" (although that is crucial). It is about feeling the physical sensation of a boundary in your body.

Notice what happens in your body when someone asks you for a favor you do not want to do. Before your mind even formulates a response, your body reacts. You might feel a tightening in your gut, a constriction in your throat, a sudden feeling of heaviness in your limbs. This is your body's "no." This is your biological boundary asserting itself.

For many of us, especially women, we have been trained to ignore this somatic "no." We have been taught to override our physical sensations in order to please others, to keep the peace, to be "good." We swallow the "no," and in doing so, we swallow the biological energy that was meant to protect us.

Rebuilding your boundaries means learning to listen to this somatic "no" and honoring it. It means allowing the physical sensation of the boundary to inform your actions. When you feel that tightening in your gut, you pause. You take a breath. You allow the sensation to be present without immediately trying to fix it or override it.

This is deeply uncomfortable work. It requires us to tolerate the anxiety of disappointing others. It requires us to face the fear of conflict. But it is essential for healing. Every time you honor your somatic "no," you are sending a powerful signal of safety to your nervous system. You are telling your body, *I am listening. I will protect you. You do not have to brace anymore.*

As you rebuild these biological boundaries, you will find that the chronic tension begins to release. The energy that was tied up in defending against intrusions becomes available for healing and restoration. The profound exhaustion begins to lift, replaced by a sense of grounded, embodied strength.

## The Wisdom of the Descent

In our culture, we are obsessed with the ascent. We value growth, productivity, and constant upward movement. We pathologize the descent - the times of rest, withdrawal, and deep restoration.

But biology operates in cycles. The inhale must be followed by the exhale. The summer must be followed by the winter. The sympathetic arousal of the day must be followed by the parasympathetic rest of the night.

The state of profound depletion we call adrenal fatigue is often the body's desperate attempt to force a descent. When we refuse to rest, when we refuse to honor the natural cycles of our biology, the body will eventually pull the emergency brake. It will enforce the descent through exhaustion, illness, and collapse.

Instead of fighting this descent, what if we surrendered to it? What if we viewed this profound exhaustion not as a failure, but as a biological imperative?

This requires a radical shift in perspective. It requires us to stop trying to "fix" our energy levels so we can get back to the frantic pace of our lives, and instead allow the exhaustion to do its work. The exhaustion is the medicine. It is the body's way of enforcing the deep, restorative rest that is required for healing.

Surrendering to the descent means allowing yourself to be unproductive. It means spending hours staring at the ceiling, feeling the weight of your bones on the mattress. It means letting the emails pile up, letting the house get messy, letting the expectations of others fall away.

It means trusting the wisdom of your body. Trusting that the descent is not a permanent state, but a necessary phase of the cycle. Trusting that when the body has received the rest it needs, the energy will naturally return.

This is the ultimate act of yielding. It is the yielding of the ego, the yielding of the need to control, the yielding to the profound, undeniable reality of our biology. In this yielding, we find the true path to healing. We find the deep, restorative nourishment that our nervous systems have been starving for. We find our way back to ourselves.

## The Cultural Gaslighting

We must also acknowledge the cultural context in which this depletion occurs. We live in a society that normalizes chronic stress and pathologizes the body's natural response to it. We are expected to work long hours, manage complex households, navigate systemic inequalities, and do it all with a smile and a perfectly curated Instagram feed.

When our bodies inevitably break down under this impossible load, we are told that we just need to practice more "self-care." We are sold expensive supplements, complicated morning routines, and the promise that if we just optimize our lives enough, we will finally have the energy we need.

This is a form of cultural gaslighting. It places the burden of healing entirely on the individual, ignoring the toxic environment that caused the harm in the first place. It tells us that our exhaustion is a personal failure, rather than a predictable biological response to an unlivable situation.

The frame of adrenal fatigue, for all its clinical inaccuracies, pushes back against this gaslighting. It insists that the problem is not in our minds, but in our bodies. It insists that the exhaustion is real, measurable, and valid.

When we claim this frame, we are engaging in a radical act of self-validation. We are saying, *I refuse to let the medical establishment or the wellness industry dictate the reality of my lived experience. I know what my body feels like. I know the depth of this depletion. And I will honor it.*

This validation is the first step toward true healing. We cannot heal what we refuse to acknowledge. By naming the exhaustion, by giving it a biological framework, we bring it out of the shadows of shame and into the light of conscious awareness.

## The Role of Anger in Depletion

We cannot talk about chronic exhaustion without talking about anger. For many women, the state of profound depletion we call adrenal fatigue is intimately connected to the suppression of anger.

Anger is a high-energy emotion. It is the biological response to a boundary violation. It is designed to give us the energy to protect ourselves, to say *no*, to push back. But we are taught from a very young age that our anger is ugly, dangerous, and unacceptable. We are taught to swallow it, to smile through it, to turn it inward.

Suppressing that much biological energy requires an immense amount of effort. It is like trying to hold a beach ball underwater. The muscles of the throat, the chest, the diaphragm, and the jaw must constantly contract to keep the anger from rising to the surface. This chronic contraction is exhausting.

When we begin to do the somatic work of unwinding our depletion, we often encounter the anger we have been holding down. As the bracing patterns begin to soften, the trapped survival energy begins to move. This can be terrifying. We may feel a sudden surge of heat in the chest, a tightening in the throat, a desire to scream or strike out.

This is where the frame of adrenal fatigue is so useful. It reminds us that this exhaustion is not a moral failing; it is a biological consequence of holding too much for too long. And it reminds us that the path to healing is not about forcing ourselves to be more productive, but about allowing the body to process the energy it has been holding.

Resmaa Menakem, in *My Grandmother's Hands*, writes about the importance of settling the nervous system and allowing the body to process historical and personal trauma. He emphasizes that healing happens in the body, not just in the mind. When we allow ourselves to feel the physical sensations of our anger - the heat, the tension, the vibration - without immediately trying to suppress it or act it out, we allow the nervous system to complete the defensive response. We allow the energy to move through us, rather than remaining trapped in our tissues.

This is not about raging at the people around us. It is about acknowledging the biological reality of our anger. It is about saying, *Yes, my boundaries were violated, and my body responded with anger. That is healthy. That is biology.*

## The Nourishment of the Nervous System

As we begin to unwind the chronic bracing and allow the trapped energy to move, we must also actively nourish the nervous system. This is where the herbs and the somatic practices work together.

We have talked about Ashwagandha and Holy Basil. But we must also consider the deep, restorative power of nervines - herbs that specifically support and tone the nervous system. 

Consider Lemon Balm. [Herb Pharm Stress Manager (1 oz)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005JW1F1C?tag=spankyspinola-20) is a gentle, uplifting nervine that helps to soothe the frayed edges of a dysregulated nervous system. It is particularly helpful for the kind of exhaustion that is accompanied by anxiety and a sense of overwhelm. It does not sedate; it simply takes the edge off the alarm bells.

And then there is the deep, restorative rest that comes from herbs like Valerian and Chamomile. When the body is so wired that it cannot drop into sleep, these herbs can provide the gentle nudge needed to transition from sympathetic arousal to parasympathetic rest. 

But herbs alone are not enough. We must also nourish the nervous system through our daily rhythms. We must begin to respect the biological need for rest, not as a reward for productivity, but as a fundamental requirement for life.

This means noticing the subtle signals of the body. When you feel that sudden drop in energy at 3:00 PM, instead of reaching for a third cup of coffee, can you lie on the floor for five minutes? Can you practice the floor yield? Can you allow your body to actually experience the tiredness, rather than constantly trying to override it?

This is the radical act of reclaiming our biology. It is the refusal to treat our bodies as machines that can be endlessly optimized. It is the recognition that we are biological organisms, subject to the rhythms of nature, the need for rest, and the physical consequences of chronic stress.

## The Slow Return of Vitality

Healing from this level of profound depletion is not a linear process. There will be days when you feel the energy returning, when the jaw softens and the breath deepens. And there will be days when the exhaustion hits you like a physical blow, when the bracing returns and the world feels overwhelming again.

This is the nature of nervous system regulation. It is a dance of expansion and contraction. The goal is not to never feel stressed again; the goal is to build the capacity to return to a state of regulation more quickly and more easily.

As you continue to practice yielding, as you continue to nourish your body with herbs and rest, you will begin to notice subtle shifts. The sudden drop in energy at 3:00 PM might become a gentle dip. The nighttime vigilance might soften into a more restful sleep. The chronic tension in your psoas might begin to release, allowing you to stand with more ease and fluidity.

You will begin to feel the slow return of vitality. Not the frantic, wired energy of sympathetic arousal, but the deep, grounded energy of a regulated nervous system. You will begin to feel the natural rhythms of your body - the ebb and flow of energy, the need for rest, the capacity for joy.

This vitality is your birthright. It is the natural state of a body that feels safe, nourished, and deeply respected. The journey back to this state is not easy, but it is the most important work you will ever do. It is the work of reclaiming your biology, your energy, and your life.

## FAQ

**Q: If my doctor says my cortisol levels are normal, does that mean my exhaustion is all in my head?**
**A:** Absolutely not. Standard blood tests measure cortisol at a single point in time and are designed to detect extreme pathology like Addison's or Cushing's disease. They do not capture the subtle, chronic dysregulation of the HPA axis, nor do they measure the immense biological cost of the chronic muscle bracing and nervous system alarm that you are experiencing. Your exhaustion is a physical reality, regardless of what the blood work says.

**Q: I feel too exhausted to even try the floor yield practice. What should I do?**
**A:** Start smaller. You do not have to get on the floor. You can practice yielding exactly where you are. If you are sitting in a chair, simply notice the contact between your thighs and the seat. Notice the contact between your back and the chair. For just one breath, see if you can let the chair hold five percent more of your weight. That is enough. The goal is not to perform a practice perfectly; the goal is to begin a conversation with your nervous system.

**Q: Can I just take the herbs you mentioned and skip the somatic practices?**
**A:** The herbs are powerful allies, but they are not a substitute for changing the underlying patterns of bracing and alarm in your body. If you take Ashwagandha but continue to suppress your anger and ignore your body's signals for rest, you are simply putting a bandage on a wound that is still bleeding. The herbs work best when they are part of a holistic approach that includes somatic awareness and a commitment to honoring your biological needs.

**Q: Why does my exhaustion feel worse when I finally stop and try to rest?**
**A:** When you are running on adrenaline and cortisol, your body is numbed to the actual depth of its depletion. It is only when you stop, when the survival chemicals begin to clear your system, that you feel the true extent of the exhaustion. This is often called the "let-down effect." It is uncomfortable, but it is a necessary part of the healing process. It means your body is finally feeling safe enough to drop the armor and reveal the truth of its condition.

**Q: Is it possible to fully recover from this level of depletion?**
**A:** Yes. The human body has an extraordinary capacity for healing and regulation. But recovery is not a return to the way you were before - the way you were before is what led to the depletion in the first place. Recovery is about building a new relationship with your body, one based on listening, yielding, and respecting your biological limits. It is a slow process, but every moment of conscious yielding, every boundary set, every deep breath is a step toward profound restoration.
